zotero.abstractNote: How do people search for rewards in environments where options are related by a graph structure? We propose a model of human search based on Gaussian process function learning over graphs. We test this model in a behavioral experiment where participants search for rewards on a graph. We find that participants' search behavior is well-described by a strategy that balances exploration and exploitation, and that they are able to leverage the graph structure to generalize rewards to unobserved nodes. Our results suggest that people use a combination of local and global information to guide their search in complex, structured environments.
zotero.proceedingsTitle: Proceedings of the 2019 Conference on Cognitive Computational Neuroscience
